title: Sparse Embeddings
concept: High-dimensional, mostly-zero vectors (like TF-IDF or learned sparse encoders) that excel at exact term matching.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we learned about dense embeddings. What is their main characteristic?

options.text: They are compact, continuous vectors where every dimension carries a signal.
They consist almost entirely of zeros to save space.
They are only used for matching exact spelling patterns.

exercises.explain: Dense embeddings pack semantic meaning into a continuous, compact vector space where every single dimension contains a value, allowing them to capture synonyms and general concepts.

cards.title: The Power of Exact Terms
cards.body: While dense embeddings are great at finding general concepts, they sometimes fail to match exact, unique words like serial numbers or product codes. That is where sparse embeddings step in to save the day.

cards.title: What is a Sparse Embedding?
cards.body: A sparse embedding is a very long vector where almost all values are zero, except for a few spots that represent the exact words present in a text. Algorithms like TF-IDF or modern learned sparse encoders use these to pinpoint exact keyword matches.

cards.title: The Giant Checklist
cards.body: Think of a sparse embedding like a massive questionnaire with millions of questions, where you only write down answers for the three things you actually own. The rest of the page is filled with zeros, leaving a clear fingerprint of your specific items.

cards.title: Exact Matching in Action
cards.body: If you search a library database for 'aquarium', a sparse embedding will have a '1' in the dimension dedicated to 'aquarium' and '0' everywhere else. It won't match 'fish tank' unless those specific words also appear in the text.

cards.title: The Myth of Slow Large Vectors
cards.body: You might think bigger vectors always take up more memory and slowdown search. However, because sparse embeddings are mostly zeros, computer systems can skip the zeros entirely, making them incredibly fast to store and search.

cards.title: Dense vs. Sparse Layouts
cards.body: Compare how dense and sparse embeddings organize their vector spaces to represent text.

left.text: Short vectors (e.g., 768 dimensions), all values filled with active numbers representing abstract concepts.

right.text: Huge vectors (e.g., 30,000+ dimensions), almost entirely zeros, highlighting exact word matches.

exercises.prompt: In which of these tasks do sparse embeddings outperform dense embeddings?

options.text: Capturing broad, abstract themes like 'happiness' without matching the word.
Matching exact words, product codes, or rare usernames.
Compressing massive books into small, two-digit coordinates.

exercises.explain: Sparse representations excel at finding exact terms and specific keywords because each unique word gets its own dedicated coordinate in the vector space.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A sparse embedding will automatically match the word 'feline' to the word 'cat' even if the text only contains the word 'cat'.
exercises.explain: Sparse embeddings are excellent at matching exact terms (like 'cat' matching 'cat'), but they do not automatically match synonyms like 'feline' unless those synonyms are explicitly present in the text or mapped beforehand.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the structure of a sparse embedding vector:
exercises.sentence: A sparse embedding relies on high-dimensional vectors that are mostly-zero.
exercises.explain: Sparse embeddings are characterized by being high-dimensional (often having tens of thousands of positions) where mostly-zero values indicate the absence of specific words.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations of sparse embeddings sounds like it was written by an AI?

options.text: Sparse embeddings are just giant checklists of words where you check off the ones that show up.
Sparse representations leverage high-dimensional vector spaces where sparsity is enforced, often utilizing learned sparse formulations like SPLADE to align term frequencies with lexical structures.
Think of it like a giant map where you only put pins in a few locations, leaving the rest completely blank.

exercises.explain: Option B is typical AI-generated text, using overly structured definitions and introducing advanced model names like SPLADE to sound authoritative. Options A and C are simple human explanations.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a search engine for a hardware store. Customers often search for exact serial codes like 'TX-990'. Which representation is better suited for finding this exact match, and why?

options.text: Dense embeddings, because they represent meanings without needing exact word matches.
Sparse embeddings, because they map exact terms to dedicated, high-dimensional coordinates.
Dense embeddings, because they use shorter vectors to speed up exact string comparison.
Sparse embeddings, because they compress all vocabulary words into a tiny, three-dimensional space.

exercises.explain: Sparse embeddings shine at capturing exact keywords like product serial numbers or rare brand names because they assign specific dimensions to unique terms. In contrast, dense embeddings compress concepts into shared dimensions, which can smudge distinct, exact search queries together.
